coredata.dc:title: Innovative aerosol technology using lemon peel-derived carbon dots for improving shelf life of beef

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (C-dots) have gained significant attention for their applications in the food industry due to their unique optical and biological properties. In this study, C-dots were synthesized from lemon peel using a hydrothermal method and their effects on beef fillets were investigated via aerosolization. Particle size analysis revealed an average C-dot size of 7.55 nm, and transmission electron microscopy confirmed the spherical shape of the dots. The radical scavenging assay indicated an antioxidant capacity with a 50 % scavenging effect of 0.66 mg/mL. Low cytotoxicity on L929 mouse fibroblasts was observed at concentrations below 0.5 mg/mL, suggesting good biocompatibility. Beef fillets were treated with C-dots at two concentrations (5 and 10 mg/mL) by aerosolization. Quality indicators (pH, total volatile basic nitrogen, thiobarbituric acid value, psychrophilic and mesophilic bacterial counts, and sensory evaluation) were measured during 15 days of refrigerated storage and showed concentration-dependent control of microbial growth and inhibition of oxidation in meat samples with 10 mg/mL proving more effective than 5 mg/mL. Both concentrations delayed spoilage by at least three days. Despite minimal color changes, sensory analysis revealed improved odor scores (characterized by smoky notes) in C-dot-treated samples. This improvement, likely due to the prevention of off-odors, led to higher overall acceptability ratings compared with the control group. These findings suggest that C-dots synthesized from lemon peel could extend the shelf life of beef fillets, and aerosolization of C-dots offers a method to reduce the amount of C-dots required while maintaining or improving the quality of beef fillets during storage.

head.ce:title: Innovative aerosol technology using lemon peel-derived carbon dots for improving shelf life of beef

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (C-dots) have gained significant attention for their applications in the food industry due to their unique optical and biological properties. In this study, C-dots were synthesized from lemon peel using a hydrothermal method and their effects on beef fillets were investigated via aerosolization. Particle size analysis revealed an average C-dot size of 7.55 nm, and transmission electron microscopy confirmed the spherical shape of the dots. The radical scavenging assay indicated an antioxidant capacity with a 50 % scavenging effect of 0.66 mg/mL. Low cytotoxicity on L929 mouse fibroblasts was observed at concentrations below 0.5 mg/mL, suggesting good biocompatibility. Beef fillets were treated with C-dots at two concentrations (5 and 10 mg/mL) by aerosolization. Quality indicators (pH, total volatile basic nitrogen, thiobarbituric acid value, psychrophilic and mesophilic bacterial counts, and sensory evaluation) were measured during 15 days of refrigerated storage and showed concentration-dependent control of microbial growth and inhibition of oxidation in meat samples with 10 mg/mL proving more effective than 5 mg/mL. Both concentrations delayed spoilage by at least three days. Despite minimal color changes, sensory analysis revealed improved odor scores (characterized by smoky notes) in C-dot-treated samples. This improvement, likely due to the prevention of off-odors, led to higher overall acceptability ratings compared with the control group. These findings suggest that C-dots synthesized from lemon peel could extend the shelf life of beef fillets, and aerosolization of C-dots offers a method to reduce the amount of C-dots required while maintaining or improving the quality of beef fillets during storage.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Foods are packaged for various reasons, including protection from harmful environmental factors (temperature, light, moisture, and oxygen), maintaining and enhancing food quality and safety, extending shelf life, and facilitating transportation (Khandeparkar et al., 2024). Active packaging takes this step further, by deliberately incorporating functional components to improve food safety and quality. Active packaging technologies can be classified into three main categories: controlled-release, antimicrobial, and antioxidant packaging (Casalini & Giacinti Baschetti, 2023; Liu et al., 2021). Antimicrobials can be delivered to the food using different techniques, such as edible films and coatings, and spray methods. In the spray technique, an antimicrobial solution is sprayed directly onto a food sample using an atomizer. High pressure creates fine droplets, ranging from micrometers to nanometers, that coat food surfaces (Priya et al., 2023). This approach is simple, low-cost, and scalable for large-scale industrial applications (Fu & Dudley, 2021).

In recent years, nanomaterials have emerged as a promising strategy for controlling microbial growth in foods. Nanostructures can be classified into four distinct categories based on their geometric confinement: zero-, one-, two-, and three-dimensional nanomaterials for food applications (Moradi et al., 2023). Carbon dots (C-dots) are a group of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials (Sattariazar et al., 2023). C-dots are special biomaterials with several applications, such as helping diagnose diseases, serving as drug delivery agents, biolabeling, bioimaging, antimicrobial and antioxidant activities (Alamdari et al., 2023), and detection of microbial and environmental contaminants (Kumar & Rhim, 2024). C-dots possess several properties that make them suitable for food preservation, including high cytocompatibility, inherent antimicrobial activity, resistance to photobleaching, and tunable luminescence (Gogoi et al., 2022). In addition, C-dots are recognized for their good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and ease of functionalization, which are essential for food safety applications (Zhao et al., 2022).

C-dots have been synthesized using various green sources, such as plant and animal wastes and microbial and bioactive molecules, which are environmentally friendly and cost-effective (Daulay et al., 2024). These sources, which are rich in carbon and other heteroatoms, serve as carbon sources and contribute to the unique properties of the resulting C-dots. The bottom-up synthesis from renewable resources aligns with green chemistry principles and expands the potential applications of C-dots in bioimaging, sensing, and catalysis (Mathew & Mathew, 2023). The type of precursor used in the synthesis of C-dots and the methods developed for their preparation can affect the properties of C-dots (Barman et al., 2021). The use of agricultural byproducts (such as skin and seeds) for the synthesis of C-dots is of significant importance for several reasons. Agricultural byproducts are abundant, renewable, and low-cost raw materials that provide an eco-friendly and sustainable alternative to non-renewable sources, such as coal and petroleum coke (Mansi et al., 2023). The synthesis of C-dots from these byproducts aligns with the principles of green chemistry, offering a method for converting waste into value-added products. Unlike synthetic preparation methods, food materials that are naturally rich in carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur are used as precursors for C-dots, eliminating the need for additional chemical reagents for doping (Moradi et al., 2023). Numerous fruits and vegetables, including banana peels (Nguyen et al., 2022), orange peels (Surendran et al., 2020), tea leaf waste, and peanut shells (Zhu et al., 2019) have been used as carbon precursors with varying degrees of success for C-dot synthesis. Citrus is a genus of the family Rutaceae comprise three genera and 18 distinct species that are broadly distributed worldwide. Citrus limon has been widely used (Harikrishnan et al., 2020). Lemon peels are not typically consumed directly; therefore, they are byproducts of lemon juice or fruit production. Lemon citrus peel waste can be used for essential oils, pectin, animal feed production, and cattle feed, either fresh or after ensilage or dehydration. However, a large amount of lemon peel is lost annually because a cost-efficient treatment process is unavailable (Boluda-Aguilar & López-Gómez, 2013; Ciriminna et al., 2020). Lime peel is rich in metabolic compounds including alkaloids, saponins, and various flavonoids (Simanjuntak & Purba, 2023). For example, Permadi et al. (2024) identified 28 components in lemon peel essential oil, with the major constituents being δ-limonene, β-pinene, citral, trans-citral, β-bisabolene, geranyl acetate, and α-pinene, by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry. Similarly, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry revealed 17 components of lemon peel extract, including nobiletin, tangeretine, scoparone, sinensetin, tetramethyl scutellarein, 5-demethylnobiletin, and pyropheophorbide A (Permadi et al., 2024). Therefore, this natural biomass inherently possesses a substantial amount of oxygen, carbon, and heteroatoms, which can be used to prepare C-dots with unique characteristics.

C-dots can be incorporated into food matrices using various methods, including direct mixing of C-dots into food (Lacivita et al., 2023). Additionally, C-dots can be encapsulated within biocompatible polymers to produce polymeric films (Min et al., 2023; Sul et al., 2023), which can be released into food upon use of the film or coating on food. In addition, pre-coating or sparing food with a C-dot solution results in the development of a layer that can inhibit microbial growth or slow down oxidation. During aerosolization, liquid antimicrobials transform into fine mist, allowing them to effectively coat the surfaces. Similar to how nebulizers deliver medications for respiratory illnesses, aerosolization creates tiny particles (approximately 5 μm or larger) that can easily reach and inactivate microorganisms (Sullivan et al., 2020). This method offers several advantages, including user-friendliness, the ability to thoroughly cover surfaces, and enhanced penetration of antimicrobial agents (Valipour et al., 2024).

In this study, C-dots were synthesized from lemon peels using a simple hydrothermal method (180 °C for 6 h), and their porphology, particle size, spectral, chemical, antimicrobial, antioxidant, and toxicity properties were characterized. These C-dots at two concentrations were applied to extend the shelf life of beef during cold storage using an aerosolization technique, which is a novel approach for delivering antimicrobials at lower concentrations than conventional methods.

Fresh beef (24 h post-mortem) was purchased from a local market in Urmia, Iran. E. coli O157: H7 (PTCC 1399) and Listeria monocytogenes (PTCC 1298) were obtained from the Iranian Research Organization for Science and Technology (IROST, Tehran, Iran). 2,4,6-Tri(2-pyridyl)−1,3,5-triazine (TPTZ) solution, ascorbic acid, sodium acetate buffer solution, peptone water, butylated hydroxytoluene (BHT), trichloroacetic acid (TCA), magnesium oxide, boric acid, sulfuric acid, HCl, FeCl3·6H2O, and Whatman filter paper were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). In addition, DPPH solution, Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium, methyl red, and methyl blue were provided by Sigma (St. Louis, USA). Plate count agar (PCA) and tyriptic soy agar were obtained from Ibesco (Karaj, Iran). The membrane syringe filter, fetal bovine serum, and thiobarbituric acid (TBA) were purchased from Millipore, MA, USA, Gibco, California, USA, and Quelab, Montreal, Canada, respectively. Dried lemons were sourced from a local store in Urmia, Iran, with fruits originating from gardens located at 28°40′29.43″ " N, 53°36′27.17″″ E, at an altitude of 1046.4 m.

C-dots were synthesized from lemon peel using a simple hydrothermal method. The peels were meticulously scraped with a knife to remove any residual fruit flesh, followed by thorough rinsing with deionized water. Afterward, the peels were oven-dried at 90 °C for 72 h until a constant weight was obtained, which had a moisture content of approximately 5.3 %. Finally, the samples were crushed and ball-milled into a fine powder. Ten grams of the powdered lemon peel was mixed with 200 mL of deionized water using a magnetic stirrer (Alfa, Tehran, Iran) at 600 rpm for 20 min. The resulting suspension was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel reactor (1 L) and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reactor contents were filtered through a Whatman filter paper 1 (Divsalar et al., 2023). The filtered product was subsequently centrifuged (Orum Tadjhiz, Tehran, Iran) at 15,000 rpm for 20 min. The resulting solution was collected and freeze-dried (Sahand Zist Farayand Tajhiz, Tabriz, Iran). Finally, the C-dot solution was re-dissolved in distilled water, filtered again through a 0.45 μm CA membrane syringe filter, and used freshly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, particle size, spectral properties and zeta potential
ce:section.ce:para: The distribution and size of the C-dots were analyzed by particle size analysis (PSA) using a scatteroscope (Qudix Inc., South Korea). Measurements were performed at 25 °C immediately after 10 min of sonication of fresh samples (without dilution). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Leo 906 E, Carl Zeiss, Germany) was performed at an accelerating voltage of 100 kV to visualize the morphology of the C-dots. The samples were diluted with deionized water (1:10), and a drop of the sample was dried on a carbon-coated Cu grid (300–400 mesh) and observed. Additionally, UV–visible spectroscopy (2000 C, Waltham, USA) was used to investigate the optical properties of the C-dots in the wavelength range of 200–700 nm with a resolution of 1 nm. For this test, 1 mg of lyophilized C-dots was suspended in 2 mL of deionized (DI) water under vigorous vortexing mixing (2 min at 25 °C). Zeta potential analysis (NanoPartica, Japan) was performed to determine the surface charge of the C-dots (refractive index =1.33, viscosity =0.8872 cP) at 25 °C. Finally, to identify the functional groups present on the C-dot surface, the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the C-dots sample was recorded using KBr pellet technique in Thermo Nicolet instrument (Nexus® 670, USA) in the 4000–450 cm−1 region with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and the obtained data have been processed in the Omnic software (version 6.0; Thermo Electron, Madison, WI, USA) (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei & Rezaei, 2022; Ghorbani et al., 2022).

The antioxidant properties of the C-dots were evaluated using two methods: DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) and FRAP (ferric reducing antioxidant power) assays. In the DPPH assay, antioxidant capacity is typically expressed as the effective concentration (IC50), which represents the amount of sample needed to reduce the initial concentration of DPPH free radicals by 50 % (Romanet et al., 2019). Here, 0.2 mL of a 4 mg/100 mL DPPH solution in methanol was added to 2 mL of C-dot solution at various concentrations (0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, and 2 mg/mL). The samples were incubated in the dark at room temperature for one hour. The absorbance of each sample was measured at 517 nm wavelength. Finally, the percentage of inhibition of the free radical scavenging effect (RS%) was calculated using Eq. (1) (Ghorbani et al., 2022). BHT was used as a control.
(1)

where Ablank and Asample represent the baseline absorbance values of the control DPPH solution and DPPH solution containing C-dots, respectively. The IC50 values were determined by plotting %RS against the concentration of C-dots and extrapolating the concentration required to achieve 50 % inhibition.

The FRAP assay reagent was prepared beforehand by mixing 10 parts of 20 mM FeCl3·6H2O solution, 1 part of 10 mM TPTZ solution Merck, and 1 part of 40 mM HCl solution at a ratio of 10:1:1 (v/v/v) in a 300 mM sodium acetate buffer solution (pH 3.6). For the assay, 80 μL of the FRAP reagent was mixed with 20 μL of C-dots solution at various concentrations (0.1, 0.5, 1, 10, and 50 mg/mL). A blank solution containing only deionized water was prepared. The mixture was incubated in the dark for 30 min at 23 ± 1 °C. The absorbance of each sample was measured at 593 nm using a spectrophotometer (Novaspec II; Pharmacia, Muttenz, Switzerland). The results are expressed as ascorbic acid equivalents (mg g-1). Higher absorbance values indicate greater reducing power of the sample, which reflects its overall antioxidant capacity. An ascorbic acid calibration curve was used to convert the absorbance values obtained from the C-dot samples to ascorbic acid equivalents (mg g-1) (Benzie & Strain, 1999).

The viability of the mouse fibroblast cell line (L929) in the presence of different concentrations of C-dots (1, 0.5, 0.25, and 0.125 mg/mL) was assessed using the MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay (Sarajar et al., 2023). The percentage of viable cells was calculated relative to the untreated control group using Eq. (2).
(2) Cell viability (%) = (mean ODtreatment/mean ODcontrol) × 100

To investigate the antimicrobial activity of C-dots against E. coli O157: H7 and L. monocytogenes, a well-diffusion assay was performed (Balouiri et al., 2016). Briefly, tyriptic soy agar plates (were prepared and inoculated with each strain (108 CFU/mL) using sterilized cotton swabs. Wells (9 mm) were punched into agar plates using a sterile cork borer. C-dot solutions at concentrations of 5, 7, and 10 mg/mL were pipetted into wells. After incubation at 37 °C for 24 h, the diameter of the clear zones surrounding the wells (zone of inhibition, ZOI) was measured and reported in millimeters.

Under sterile conditions, the meat was transported to the laboratory and cut into pieces (6 × 9 × 1 cm; 50 ± 2 g). The meat pieces were then placed in a treatment box for aerosolizations (Fig. 1 ). Separate sterile C-dot solutions were prepared at concentrations of 5 and 10 mg/mL. The concentration selection was based on the results of a separate antimicrobial experiment on meat (data not shown). These solutions were carefully loaded into the reservoir of a commercial nebulizer (3A; Lonato del Garda, Italy) (Fig. 1). The aerosolizations process was conducted within a treatment box with a capacity of 7.5 L. The box measured 26.5 cm in length, 21 cm wide, and 17.5 cm high. It is equipped with two ventilation ports, each with a diameter of 2 cm. The inlet port was positioned 13 cm from the base of the box and the outlet port was located 2 cm higher. Fifty grams of meat cuts were placed on a sterile perforated grill tray situated 10 cm above the base of the chamber and 1 cm from the inlet port. The nebulizer was then switched on to spray a fine mist of the C-dot solution onto the meat surface, until the entire solution was aerosolized. Following aerosolization treatment for 20 min (Sullivan et al., 2020), both treated and untreated meat samples were individually packaged using the overwrapped tray method within polyethylene containers. The samples were stored at 7 °C for 15 days. Subsequently, microbiological, chemical, and sensory analyses were conducted.

Sensory evaluation of the treated meat samples was conducted over 15 d of storage. Panelists (n = 9) used a 9-point hedonic test to assess color, odor, and overall acceptability. The panelists were semi-trained and evaluated using coded labels to ensure blinding. Prior to the sensory test, each participant provided informed consent. Although no formal ethics committee or documentation was available, the authors ensured that proper protocols were followed to protect participants' rights and privacy throughout the test. Lovibond® LC 100 Spectrocolorimeter (Tintometer® Group, Lovibond house, UK) was used detremine color parameters of meat (Ghorbani et al., 2024).

Ten grams of each meat sample were mixed with 90 mL of 0.1 % peptone water on days 0, 3, 6, 9, 12, and 15. The mixture was stomached for 3 min at 260 rpm using a stomacher (Seward Medical Ltd. London, U.K). Subsequently, 1:10 dilutions were prepared and cultured on PCA agar to determine the total mesophilic count (TMC) and total psychrophilic count (TPC). The plates for TMC were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, whereas the plates for TPC were incubated at 10 °C for seven days (Ceylan et al., 2023).

TBARS assay was used to quantify lipid oxidation in the samples (Bazargani-Gilani et al., 2015). Briefly, 10 g of meat was homogenized in 1 mL of BHT solution (1 mg/mL) and 35 mL of a 4 % TCA solution. The homogenate was then filtered through a Whatman No 42 filter paper. The filtrate volume was adjusted to 50 mL using 4 % TCA solution. 5 mL of the filtrate were mixed with 10 mL of 0.02 M TBA reagent and incubated in a water bath at 90 °C for 1 h. After cooling to 40 °C, the absorbance of the samples was measured at 532 nm using a spectrophotometer against a blank containing 2 mL of 0.02 M TBA reagent and 2 mL 4 % TCA solution. Malondialdehyde (MDA) concentration in the samples was calculated from a standard curve of MDA and expressed as milligrams of MDA per kilogram of meat.
TVB-N content in the samples was determined using the Kjeldahl method (Mirmoeini et al., 2023).
The pH of homogenized meat samples was determined using a pH meter (Metrohm, Herisau, Switzerland).

One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to analyze the data. Subsequently, Tukey's test was used to identify statistically significant differences in the means between groups at a significance level of 5 % (P < 0.5). All analyses were conducted using GraphPad Prism version 8.01 for Windows (San Diego, CA, USA).

The type of precursor and synthesis conditions (i.e., temperature and heating time) can affect the C-dot specifications, which ultimately leads to different colors of the C-dots, with emission from blue to red (Hua et al., 2017). The C-dots showed bright green emission under UV light (Fig. 2 ), which is considered an indicator of the successful synthesis of C-dots (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi et al., 2022). In the UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 2), the indexed peak at 284 nm is evidence of electron transitions of π–π hybrid orbitals associated with C = C-and C = N-conjugated functional groups. In addition, a relatively weak absorption region (called a tail-like peak) in the range 315–340 nm is related to n–*π transitions in the CO and–NH2 groups (Ghorbani et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size, dispersity, morphology and zeta potential of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The size, shape, and zeta potential of C-dots significantly affect their functionality (Moradi et al., 2023). The distribution and particle size of the C-dots synthesized from lemon peel were assessed using PSA, and their morphologies were analyzed using TEM. According to the histogram of particle size distribution (Fig. 3 ), over 95 % of the particles were smaller than 12.8 nm, with an average size of 7.5 nm. Additionally, TEM images revealed that the synthesized C-dots exhibited a spherical morphology. Nanostructures generally exhibit high surface-to-volume ratio. However, when they become unstable and accumulate, their activities decrease. This underscores the necessity of examining the surface loads to better understand and evaluate their stability (Shankar et al., 2018). Subsequently, a zeta potential analysis was performed to determine the surface charge of the C-dots. The zeta potential of the synthesized C-dots was −15.3 mV. This negative charge accounts for the repulsion between particles, leading to relatively good dispersion and contributing to the stability of nanoparticle performance to some extent. The negative charge was likely due to the presence of negatively charged groups on the surface of the C-dots, such as hydroxyl and carbonyl groups, as confirmed by the FTIR analysis (Gao et al., 2019). Particles with zeta potential values in the range of −25 mV to +25 mV are considered to have good stability (Molaei et al., 2018). C-dots with different zeta potentials were synthesized from baker's yeast-derived compounds (−47.6 mV) (Ghorbani et al., 2022), acetate and zinc (−11.4 mV) (Divsalar et al., 2023), sour whey (−25 mV) (Esmaeili Koutamehr et al., 2023), and date seeds (−6.74 mV) (Alamdari et al., 2023).

FTIR spectroscopy was used to determine the functional groups of the C-dots (Fig. 4 ). Accordingly, the strong absorption peak centered at 3387 cm−1 can be attributed to the hydroxyl (OH) or amine (NH) groups, and the bifurcated bands at 2922 and 2859 cm−1 can be attributed to the vibrations of the CH bonds in the methyl and methylene groups in the synthesized structure (Ghorbani et al., 2022). The strong band at 1587 cm−1 confirmed the presence of a carbonyl functional group (C = O) (Shafipour Yordshahi et al., 2020). 1410 cm−1 and 1228 cm−1 can be attributed to CO and CN vibrations (Esmaeili Koutamehr et al., 2023). In addition, the absorption bands at 1112 cm−1 and 1043 cm−1 and the multi-branched bands in the range of 805–685 cm−1 wave numbers are evidence of the vibrations of CC and CH bonds in the aromatic rings (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi et al., 2022).

In this study, an MTT assay was performed on normal L929 cells to investigate the toxic effects of different concentrations of the synthesized C-dots. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , 95 % of the cells survived at C-dot concentrations < 0.5 mg/mL. The findings of this study were compared to those of Zhao et al. (2021) (C-dots from seaweed with >80 % survival at concentation higher than 360 µg/mL) and Jayasekhar Babu et al. (2021) (C-dots from seaweed with 85 % survival at concentrations 100 µg/mL) indicate that C-dots synthesized from lemon peel exhibit low toxicity on the L929 mouse fibroblast cell line. The differences in the results of different studies could be attributed to factors such as size, surface charge, and surface functional groups on the C-dots, which can affect their toxicity (Yan et al., 2018). The relationship between the C-dot size and toxicity is complex and not fully understood. While smaller nanoparticles tend to have an increased potential to interact with biological systems, other factors, such as surface chemistry, dosage, and synthesis method, play a more significant role in determining C-dot toxicity (Chung et al., 2021). Kuznietsova et al. (2023) suggested that surface properties may be more crucial than size in determining C-dots toxicity. The surface charge significantly influences the inflammatory response of C-dots, with positively charged C-dots inducing the release of inflammatory cytokines, whereas negatively charged C-dots (as synthesized in the current study) do not trigger such a response (Usman et al., 2020). Additionally, the surface properties and functional groups of C-dots also influence their interactions with cells. Smaller C-dots can be functionalized with various molecules, which affects their toxicity profile. Xie et al. (2019) investigated the cytotoxicity of different types of C-dots, including COOH-graphene quantum dots, OH-graphene quantum dots, and NH2-graphene quantum dots. They found that OH-graphene quantum dots were the most toxic, inducing significant cell death at a concentration of 100 μg/mL. In contrast, COOH- and NH2-graphene quantum dots were non-cytotoxic at the same concentration.

The antioxidant activity of C-dots was assessed using DPPH and FRAP assays. Lemon peel-derived C-dots demonstrated free radical scavenging activity in the DPPH assay, with an IC50 value of 0.66 mg/mL (Fig. 6 ), indicating their potential as antioxidants. The free radical scavenging capacity of C-dots varies based on factors such as their size, surface chemistry, and preparation methods. Some studies have reported IC50 values lower than 0.1 mg/mL for C-dots synthesized from pomegranate peel, indicating high antioxidant activity (Sattariazar et al., 2023). In contrast, IC50 values exceeding 5 mg/mL, which indicate lower radical scavenging activity, have also been reported for C-dots synthesized from biowaste peels (Rajamanikandan et al., 2022). The lower antioxidant activity of C-dots synthesized from lemon peel could be due to their limited reaction with free radicals in solution or excessive production of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) (Ghorbani et al., 2022).

FRAP assay was used to investigate the total antioxidant capacity of the C-dots. In FRAP, the Fe+3-TPTZ complex turns into colored bivalent iron (Fe+2) because of the reaction with antioxidant agents and hydrogen capture in the sample. Based on these results (Fig. 6), the antioxidant activity of C-dots increased as their concentration rises from 0.1 to 10 mg/mL. Concentration-dependent FRAP activity has commonly been observed in antioxidant studies of C-dots (Divsalar et al., 2023; Shabbir et al., 2022). Higher C-dot concentrations provided more electron donors, leading to greater reduction in ferric ions.

The antioxidant activity of C-dots is influenced by several factors, including their structure, surface functional groups, and composition (Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023). The specific structure of C-dots, such as their size and shape, has been shown to affect their radical scavenging activities (Cheng et al., 2018). Interestingly, the synthesis method and applied carbon source can also affect the antioxidant capabilities of C-dots. For instance, C-dots synthesized from tomato juice exhibit a higher antioxidant power than other sources, which is attributed to the natural antioxidants present in the fruit (Rodríguez-Varillas et al., 2022). The antioxidant effect observed in the C-dots synthesized in this study can be attributed to their ability to participate in electron-transfer reactions. This electron transfer enables the C-dots to effectively neutralize free radicals, forming the basis of their antioxidant mechanism (Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023).

C-dots are novel nano-based antimicrobials that can control and eliminate bacterial infections with relatively few toxic side effects on mammalian cells and tissues. This activity is likely related to C-dots disrupting the cell wall or membrane, producing reactive oxygen species (ROS), and damaging DNA (Lin et al., 2022). In this study, the agar-based antibacterial activity of C-dots was investigated. Fig. 7 shows the zones of inhibition for L. monocytogenes and E. coli at different C-dots concentrations. The zone of inhibition for L. monocytogenes at concentrations of 50, 75, and 100 mg/mL of C-dots is 13.8 ± 1.3, 17.9 ± 2.3, and 24.8 ± 2.1 mm, respectively, while in E. coli, the zones of inhibition were 13.8 ± 1.8, 16.7 ± 1.5, and 20.7 ± 2.8 mm, respectively. These results confirmed that the antibacterial performance was concentration-dependent and that both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria were similarly sensitive, although L. monocytogenes was more sensitive. The antibacterial potential of C-dots is highly dependent on their surface charge and ROS generation (Divsalar et al., 2023). This explains the observed differences in the antimicrobial properties of C-dots reported in the literature. For example, C-dots with different surface charges—negative (−19.5 mV) from candle soot, positive (+27.6 mV) from spermine, and nearly neutral (0.94 mV) from glucose—demonstrated varying performance on E. coli (Bing et al., 2016). Owing to the differences in zeta potential, L. monocytogenes (−2.5 mV) (Wang et al., 2014) is expected to interact more favorably with the synthesized C-dots (−15.3 mV) than with E. coli (−4.95 mV) (Kim & Harrison, 2016). This revealed a weaker repulsion between the C-dots and both bacteria.

Meat spoilage is characterized by discoloration, bad smell, slime, and leakage, making it unappealing. Bacteria are the main culprit behind meat spoilage compared to other factors such as enzymes or oxidation (Bekhit et al., 2021). In the current study, the effect of two different C-dots concentrations used in aerosolized form on beef quality during 15 days of refrigerated storage was investigated. Compared to traditional methods such as spraying or dipping, aerosolization has several advantages for applying antimicrobial C-dots to meat. This technique creates a fine mist that uniformly coats the entire surface, ensuring that all areas are exposed to C-dots, potentially leading to more effective bacterial control. Additionally, aerosolization minimizes waste by using fewer antimicrobial solutions and allows for deeper penetration into the meat matrix, reaching bacteria that might be missed by other methods. Furthermore, the process can be easily automated, allowing consistent and efficient application across large batches of meat in commercial settings (Sullivan et al., 2020; Torrijos et al., 2022; Valipour et al., 2024; Zhao et al., 2022).

The inhibitory effects of aerosolized C-dots synthesized from lemon peel on TMC and TPC of meat were investigated (Table 1 ). The TMC increased from 3.2 to 8.1 log10 CFU/g in the control group during 15 days of storage. However, the rate of increase (slope) was slower for the treated samples with C-dots aerosolization than that in the control group. TMC in the treatment group at a concentration of 10 mg/mL was significantly lower (P < 0.05) than that in the control group on days 3, 6, 9, 12, and 15. Similarly, the 5 mg/mL C-dot treatment group showed significantly lower TMC than the control group on days 12 and 15 (P < 0.05).

Similar to TMC, TPC increased during storage for 15 d; however, the increase was lower in the C-dot-treated groups than in the control group (Table 1). This indicated the effectiveness of C-dots against the microbiota present in this type of meat. The growth of psychrophilic bacteria mirrored the trend observed for the TMC. In the 10 mg/mL C-dots treatment group, TPC was significantly lower (P < 0.05) than that in the control group on days 3, 6, 9, 12, and 15. Similarly, the 5 mg/mL C-dot treatment group showed significantly lower (P < 0.05) growth of psychrophiles on days 6, 9, 12, and 15 than the control. According to existing standards, the maximum acceptable TMC in fresh meat is 7 log10 CFU/g (Tometri et al., 2020). In the control group, the TMC limit was reached on day 12; however, the 5 mg/mL and 10 mg/mL C-dot-treated meat samples exceeded this limit on days 12 and 15, respectively. This suggests that C-dots, particularly at a concentration of 10 mg/mL, may be effective in extending the shelf life of meat during storage.

The pH of stored meat gradually increases owing to microbial protein degradation, releasing amino acids that form alkaline compounds such as NH3 and amines (Wang et al., 2023). Table 1 shows an initial pH of 5.81, which increased during storage. However, both C-dot-treated groups initially displayed a decrease in pH owing to their acidic properties. As the microbial load increased, the pH increased again, but at a slower rate than that in the control group. Among the treatments, the group treated with 5 mg/mL C-dots showed a slightly higher final pH than the 10 mg/mL group. In a study by Ghorbani et al. (2024), minced beef packaged with nanocellular antimicrobial films containing C-dots synthesized from microbial sources showed a slower increase in pH than that of the control group during storage.

The antimicrobial properties of C-dots can inhibit the growth of spoilage microorganisms, thereby reducing the production of nitrogenous compounds and resulting in a lower TVB-N (Ghorbani et al., 2024). The initial TVB-N was 2.5 mg/ 100 g, which indicated the freshness of the meat (Table 1). The amount of TVB-N increased over time. Meat treated with C-dots at a concentration of 10 mg/mL had significantly (P < 0.05) lower values on days 6, 9, 12, and 15. The treatment at a concentration of 5 mg/mL showed significantly lower values (P < 0.05) compared to the control on days 9, 12, and 15. Based on these results, it can be concluded that the antibacterial effects of C-dots on meat spoilers were able to retard bacterial growth and increase meat quality in the treatment group compared with the control group after 15 days.

The effects of C-dots on meat TBARS are likely to be complex and may vary depending on factors, such as C-dot specification, meat type, and method of application (Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023). For example, the high reactivity of C-dots, owing to their nanostructure, may increase TBARS levels in meat, a marker of oxidative stress. However, some C-dots possess antioxidant properties, potentially lowering TBARS levels (Ghorbani et al., 2024). As shown in Table 1, the TBARS of 0.3 malondialdehyde (MDA)/kg was initially measured. It increased over time in both the control and treatment groups. However, the rate of increase (slope) was lower in the treatment groups than in the control group, likely due to the free radical-inhibiting ability of the synthesized C-dots, which can reduce the degree of lipid oxidation. Treatments with different concentrations showed significant differences (P < 0.05) compared with the control sample on days 12 and 15. Fu et al. (2022) used C-dots from chitosan to develop a fish-packaging film. Similar to the present study, their film slowed down the increase in spoilage indicators (including TBARS) in fish stored at room temperature for 10 days.

This study investigated the use of C-dots synthesized from lemon peel as antimicrobial agents in beef. The physicochemical properties of the C-dots were characterized using various analytical techniques. The C-dots exhibited a spherical morphology with an average size of 5.7 nm and a zeta potential of −15.7 mV, indicating stability of nanomaterials. The C-dots exhibited antioxidant activity, with an IC50 value of 0.66 mg/mL. Antimicrobial assays have demonstrated effective antibacterial activities against a range of Gram-positive and Gram-negative foodborne pathogens. The performance of aerosolized C-dots as a novel method for antimicrobial application in food was investigated. C-dots improved all quality parameters of meat in a concentration-dependent manner, with 10 mg/mL being more effective than 5 mg/mL. Both 5 and 10 mg/mL aerosolized C-dots delayed meat spoilage by at least three days. The most important finding was the use of a lower amount of C-dots through aerosolization compared to other delivery methods, which can help reduce costs and improve effectiveness. The use of aerosolization to deliver antimicrobials, including C-dots, at a commercial scale is recommended.
This manuscript does not involve research with human or animal subjects.

Ehsan Semsari: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Hossein Tajik: Visualization, Supervision. Rahim Molaei: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Mehran Moradi: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
